text: For intermittent internet dropout: First check for outages in the customer's area via the outage map. If no outage, check line signal levels remotely — if downstream power is below -7 dBmV or upstream above 48 dBmV, schedule a technician. Otherwise, instruct the customer to reboot the modem: unplug power for 60 seconds, replug, wait 2 minutes. If dropout persists, check for splitters or coax cable damage.

For slow internet speeds: Run a remote speed test from the modem portal before asking the customer to test. If modem-side speeds are normal but customer-side are low, the issue is internal (router, WiFi, device). If modem speeds are also low, check T3/T4 timeout errors in the modem event log — these indicate line quality issues. For consistent slowdowns during peak hours (6–9 PM), the node may be congested — escalate to network ops.

For reported outages: Check the outage management system before any troubleshooting. If there is an active outage in the customer's node, acknowledge it, provide the estimated restore time, and offer a prorated bill credit for the outage duration. Do not ask customers to troubleshoot during a confirmed outage — it wastes time and increases handle time.

For unexpected charges on the bill: Pull the account billing history and identify the charge type. Common causes: (1) promo rate expired — the rate reverts to standard; (2) equipment fee added after promo waiver ended; (3) one-time technician visit fee ($75–$99) for issues found to be inside the home. For promo expirations, offer a 6-month retention rate if the account is in good standing (no more than 1 late payment in 12 months).

For billing credits: Agents can issue a prorated credit for confirmed outages without supervisor approval up to $50. Credits over $50 require tier-2 approval. Credits for promo disputes require account review — check if the customer was notified of the promo end date via email (system logs this). If notification was sent, the credit is discretionary; if no notification was logged, issue the credit automatically.

For plan upgrades: Check the account for upgrade eligibility — customers must be 90+ days on the current plan and have no outstanding balance. Present the next tier plan with monthly cost difference and speed improvement. Confirm the upgrade takes effect on the next billing cycle (not immediately). Email confirmation is auto-sent. No equipment change required unless upgrading above 500 Mbps, which requires a DOCSIS 3.1 modem.

For plan downgrades: Customers can downgrade once per 12-month period. Warn that a downgrade from a promotional bundle may trigger loss of the promotional pricing on the remaining services (e.g., downgrading internet may reset the TV promo). Confirm effective date is next billing cycle. Offer the lowest eligible retention rate before processing the downgrade.

For new SIM activation: The SIM must be inserted before calling. Verify the ICCID on the SIM card matches the order. Activate via the provisioning portal using the account number and ICCID. After activation, the customer must toggle airplane mode off/on to force network registration. If the phone still shows 'No Service' after 5 minutes, check if the IMEI is unlocked — locked IMEIs from other carriers will not activate.

For number porting (transferring a number from another carrier): The customer must NOT cancel their existing service before the port completes — canceling early releases the number. Port completion takes 2–4 hours for mobile-to-mobile, up to 3 business days for landline ports. Customers need: their existing account number, billing PIN, and the exact name and address on the account with the losing carrier. Port status can be checked in the provisioning portal.

For phone network connectivity issues (calls dropping, no data): Start by toggling airplane mode. If unresolved, reset APN settings to carrier defaults (do not do a full network reset as this clears saved WiFi passwords and Bluetooth pairings unnecessarily). If the issue persists on one device only, the problem is likely device-side. If multiple devices are affected, check for node-level issues or provisioning errors on the account.

For modem/router issues: Check the modem event log for T3 timeouts (upstream ranging failures) and T4 timeouts (upstream bonding failures) — these indicate outside plant issues, not the modem itself. A modem with 10+ T3 errors in 24 hours should trigger a technician visit. For modem lights: solid green = normal, blinking green = online activity, solid amber = limited connectivity, solid red = no signal. A firmware update can resolve some instability — trigger remotely via the device management portal.

For equipment returns: Generate a prepaid return label from the equipment management portal. The customer has 14 days to return equipment after cancellation or equipment swap to avoid a non-return fee ($150 for modem, $100 for router). Unreturned equipment fees are charged to the final bill. If a customer claims they already returned equipment, check the warehouse receipt log — it can take 5–7 business days to process returns.

For scheduling technician visits: Available appointment windows are 8AM–12PM and 12PM–5PM. Next available is visible in the scheduling portal. Technician visits are free if the issue is found to be outside the home (line, tap, node). If the fault is inside the home (internal wiring, customer-owned splitters, customer-provided router), a $75–$99 service fee applies. Inform the customer of this before scheduling to avoid bill disputes.
